name: Malta
tagline: Crossroads of the Mediterranean, from the world's oldest temples to a modern European republic.
summary: Malta's history is the history of a tiny but coveted archipelago at the centre of the Mediterranean, fought over and shaped by every power that dominated the sea around it. Its earliest inhabitants raised the megalithic temples of Ggantija, Hagar Qim, Mnajdra and Tarxien between roughly 3600 and 2500 BCE, monumental stone buildings that rank among the oldest free-standing structures on Earth, and hollowed out the subterranean necropolis of the Hal Saflieni Hypogeum. Phoenician traders and then Carthage held the islands before Rome took them in 218 BCE; Christian tradition dates the islands' conversion to the shipwreck of the Apostle Paul around 60 CE. Arab rule from 870 gave Malta its Semitic language, the Normans brought it into the orbit of Sicily in 1091, and in 1530 the Emperor Charles V handed the islands to the Knights of St John. The Knights' repulse of the Ottomans in the Great Siege of 1565 and the founding of Valletta made Malta a Christian bastion. Napoleon seized the islands in 1798, but a Maltese revolt and British power confirmed British rule by the Treaty of Paris in 1814, turning Malta into a great naval base whose heroism under siege in the Second World War won the whole island the George Cross. Malta gained independence in 1964, became a republic in 1974 and a neutral state after the British left in 1979, and joined the European Union in 2004.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: Modern Malta dates its statehood to 21 September 1964, when the islands became an independent sovereign state within the Commonwealth after more than a century and a half of British colonial rule. Independence followed centuries in which Malta had been governed in turn by Carthage, Rome, the Arabs, the Normans of Sicily, the Knights of St John, France and finally Britain, which had confirmed its possession of the islands by the Treaty of Paris in 1814. The independent State of Malta initially retained the British monarch as head of state; it became a republic on 13 December 1974, and after the departure of the last British forces on 31 March 1979 it adopted a policy of constitutional neutrality, completing its passage from imperial naval base to a small, self-governing European nation that joined the European Union in 2004.

quickFacts.label: Oldest monuments
quickFacts.value: Megalithic temples of Ggantija and others, c. 3600–2500 BCE, among the world's oldest free-standing structures

quickFacts.label: Granted to the Knights
quickFacts.value: Emperor Charles V gave Malta to the Order of St John in 1530

quickFacts.label: The Great Siege
quickFacts.value: Knights under Jean Parisot de la Valette repulsed the Ottomans in 1565; Valletta founded 1566

quickFacts.label: George Cross
quickFacts.value: Awarded to the whole island by King George VI on 15 April 1942 for its endurance in the Second World War

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From the United Kingdom on 21 September 1964; a republic from 13 December 1974

quickFacts.label: European Union
quickFacts.value: Joined the EU on 1 May 2004 and adopted the euro on 1 January 2008

eras.title: The Temple Builders and Prehistoric Malta
eras.standfirst: On two small islands a Neolithic people raised some of the oldest monumental architecture on Earth, then vanished, leaving stone temples and an underground city of the dead.
body: Malta was first settled by farmers crossing from Sicily in the sixth millennium BCE, but it was their descendants who left the islands their most extraordinary inheritance. Between roughly 3600 and 2500 BCE a Neolithic society without metal tools, working with chert, flint and antler, raised a series of monumental stone temples across Malta and Gozo. The two temples of Ggantija on Gozo, whose South Temple was built around 3600 BCE, are among the earliest free-standing monumental structures anywhere in the world, older than the pyramids of Egypt and the stone circle of Stonehenge.

On the larger island the temples of Hagar Qim, Mnajdra and Tarxien display a refined architecture of massive uprights, corbelled chambers and curving apses, while the smaller complexes of Ta' Hagrat and Skorba show how the tradition was handed down across generations. Archaeologists divide the era into successive phases named after these sites, the Ggantija and Tarxien phases among them, and the buildings yield evidence of animal sacrifice, spiral-decorated pottery and corpulent figurines often interpreted as deities of fertility or of the dead.

The most haunting monument is underground. The Hal Saflieni Hypogeum, cut on three levels into the soft limestone of what is now Paola, was a subterranean necropolis in which the remains of some 7,000 individuals were found, many of their bones coloured with red ochre. Among its treasures is the so-called Sleeping Lady, a small clay figure of a reclining woman about five thousand years old. Then, around 2500 BCE, the temple-building culture disappeared, for reasons still debated, leaving the stones to a new and less monumental Bronze Age population.

events.title: The temples of Ggantija raised on Gozo
events.summary: The two megalithic temples of Ggantija were built on Gozo, the South Temple around 3600 BCE, ranking among the oldest free-standing monumental buildings in the world.

events.title: The Maltese temple culture flourishes
events.summary: A Neolithic society raised the temples of Hagar Qim, Mnajdra, Tarxien, Skorba and Ta' Hagrat in successive phases, creating monumental architecture without the use of metal tools.

events.title: The Hal Saflieni Hypogeum
events.summary: A vast underground necropolis was cut on three levels into the limestone, holding the remains of about 7,000 people and the famous Sleeping Lady figurine.

events.title: The temple builders disappear
events.summary: Around 2500 BCE the temple-building culture came to an end for reasons still debated, and a new Bronze Age population succeeded the people who had raised the great monuments.

figures.name: The temple builders of Malta
figures.blurb: An anonymous farming people who, without metal or the wheel, quarried and raised the megalithic temples and carved the Hal Saflieni Hypogeum, leaving some of the earliest monumental architecture on Earth before their culture vanished around 2500 BCE.

figures.name: The Sleeping Lady
figures.blurb: A small reclining clay figure recovered from the Hal Saflieni Hypogeum, traced with red ochre and roughly five thousand years old, widely regarded as a masterpiece of prehistoric art and a possible image of a mother goddess or of eternal sleep.

pullquote.text: The temples of Malta rank amongst the oldest free-standing buildings in the world, raised by a people who had neither metal tools nor the wheel.

eras.title: Phoenicians, Carthage, Rome and the Coming of Christianity
eras.standfirst: A natural way-station between Africa, Sicily and the Levant, Malta passed from Phoenician traders to Carthage and Rome, and tradition makes it one of the first lands won for Christianity.
body: Malta's position in the narrows of the Mediterranean drew the great trading powers of antiquity. Phoenician seafarers used the islands as a station on their routes between the Levant and the western Mediterranean, and by the sixth century BCE the Carthaginians, heirs to the Phoenician world, had clearly established themselves there; coins, inscriptions and Punic-type rock tombs attest to their long presence. The islands lay within Carthage's maritime sphere until the contest between Carthage and Rome decided their fate.

In 218 BCE, during the Second Punic War, Malta came under Roman control and was attached to the praetorship of Sicily. Under Rome the islands prospered: for their first two centuries within the empire the Maltese were allowed to coin their own money, send delegations to Rome and manage their domestic affairs, and the islands were later granted the rank of a Roman municipium. Roman villas, harbours and a Greek-influenced urban culture flourished on what had become a settled, integrated province.

It is to this Roman period that Christian tradition assigns Malta's conversion. According to the Acts of the Apostles, the Apostle Paul was shipwrecked on the island around 60 CE while being taken as a prisoner to Rome, was welcomed by the islanders and their leading man Publius, and stayed about three months, during which Publius and many Maltese were converted. Whatever the precise history, the tradition made Malta one of the earliest Christian communities and rooted on the islands a Catholic identity that has endured for two millennia. Roman rule gave way to Byzantine, under which Malta remained until the Arab conquest of 870.

events.title: Carthaginian Malta
events.summary: Following earlier Phoenician use of the islands as a trading station, the Carthaginians were firmly established in Malta by the sixth century BCE, leaving coins, inscriptions and Punic rock tombs.

events.title: Malta becomes Roman
events.summary: During the Second Punic War Malta passed under Roman control and was attached to the praetorship of Sicily, beginning some seven centuries of Roman and then Byzantine rule.

events.title: The shipwreck of the Apostle Paul
events.summary: Christian tradition, drawing on the Acts of the Apostles, holds that Paul was shipwrecked on Malta around 60 CE, welcomed by the islanders and the leading man Publius, and stayed three months, beginning the islands' conversion.

events.title: Roman municipium
events.summary: Under Rome the Maltese were allowed to coin money, send delegations to Rome and manage their own affairs, and the islands were granted the status of a Roman municipium.

figures.name: Paul the Apostle
figures.blurb: According to the Acts of the Apostles, Paul was shipwrecked on Malta around 60 CE on his voyage to Rome. The islanders' kindness and his stay of about three months, during which Publius and others were converted, became the founding story of Maltese Christianity.

figures.name: Publius
figures.role: Roman official, traditional first bishop of Malta
figures.blurb: Described in the Acts of the Apostles as the leading man of the island who received Paul hospitably; after Paul healed his father, tradition holds that Publius converted to Christianity and became the first bishop of Malta.

pullquote.text: After we had reached safety, we then learned that the island was called Malta. The natives showed us unusual kindness.
pullquote.attribution: Acts of the Apostles 28, on Paul's shipwreck

eras.title: Arabs, Normans and Medieval Malta
eras.standfirst: Arab rule gave Malta its Semitic language; the Normans bound it to Sicily, and for four centuries the islands were a frontier outpost of the Latin kingdoms of the central Mediterranean.
body: In 870 an Arab force, part of the Aghlabid expansion out of North Africa that had already taken Sicily, seized Malta from the Byzantine Empire. The Arab period, which lasted more than two centuries, was decisive for the islands' identity in one lasting respect: it gave Malta its language. Maltese descends from the Arabic spoken by these settlers and is the only Semitic language written in the Latin alphabet, a unique survival that still carries the imprint of those centuries even after the islands were drawn back into Christian Europe.

That reorientation began in 1091, when the Norman count Roger I of Sicily took Malta from the Arabs, completing the Norman conquest of the central Mediterranean. The islands became an appendage of the Kingdom of Sicily and shared its fortunes through successive dynasties, the Norman Hautevilles, the Swabian Hohenstaufen, the Angevins and from the late thirteenth century the house of Aragon. A Muslim population persisted under early Norman rule but was steadily reduced, and Malta was progressively re-Christianised and tied ever more closely to Sicily and the western Mediterranean.

For these medieval centuries Malta was a minor frontier territory, governed through feudal lords and a local council, the Universita, and exposed to raids by corsairs from North Africa. Its small population looked to Sicily for protection and trade. This long subordination to the Crown of Aragon and its successors ended only in 1530, when the Emperor Charles V, who as king of Spain also ruled Sicily, granted the islands to a homeless crusading order in search of a new base.

events.title: Arab conquest of Malta
events.summary: An Arab force seized Malta from the Byzantine Empire, beginning a period of more than two centuries that would shape the islands' language and culture.

events.title: Birth of the Maltese language
events.summary: The Arabic of the islands' settlers evolved into Maltese, a Semitic language that is unique in being written in the Latin alphabet and that survives as the national language today.

events.title: Norman conquest by Roger I
events.summary: Count Roger I of Sicily took Malta from the Arabs in 1091, attaching the islands to the Kingdom of Sicily and beginning their return to Latin Christendom.

events.title: Rule under Sicily and Aragon
events.summary: As part of the Kingdom of Sicily, Malta passed through Norman, Swabian, Angevin and Aragonese hands, governed by feudal lords and a local council, until 1530.

figures.name: Roger I of Sicily
figures.blurb: The Norman conqueror who completed the seizure of Sicily and took Malta from the Arabs in 1091, drawing the islands into the Latin Christian world and the orbit of the Sicilian crown.

figures.name: The Maltese language
figures.blurb: Rooted in the Arabic of the islands' settlers after 870, Maltese is the only Semitic language written in the Latin alphabet. It absorbed Italian, Sicilian and later English influences yet preserves its Arabic core, an enduring legacy of the medieval centuries.

pullquote.text: Maltese, rooted in the Arabic introduced after 870, is the only Semitic language written in the Latin alphabet, a unique survival of Malta's medieval crossroads.
pullquote.attribution: On the legacy of Arab rule

eras.title: The Knights of St John and the Great Siege
eras.standfirst: Granted to a crusading order that had lost Rhodes, Malta became a fortress of Christendom; the repulse of the Ottomans in 1565 and the building of Valletta made the islands famous across Europe.
body: The Knights of the Order of the Hospital of St John of Jerusalem, founded in the early twelfth century to care for pilgrims in the Holy Land and long established as a sovereign military order on Rhodes, had been driven from that island by the Ottomans in 1522. Homeless for eight years, the Order received Malta in 1530 from the Emperor Charles V, who as king of Spain held Sicily, in return for a token yearly tribute. The Knights made the Grand Harbour their base and resumed their maritime war against Ottoman and Barbary shipping, drawing the hostility of the most powerful empire in the Mediterranean.

The reckoning came in 1565. A great Ottoman expedition sent by Suleiman the Magnificent landed on Malta in May and laid siege to the Knights and the Maltese for nearly four months. The defenders, commanded by the aged Grand Master Jean Parisot de la Valette, held the harbour forts against overwhelming numbers; Fort St Elmo fell only after a murderous defence, and the garrison endured fearful losses before a relief force from Sicily forced the Ottomans to withdraw in September. The Great Siege of 1565 checked Ottoman expansion in the central Mediterranean and made Malta a symbol of Christian resistance.

In the wake of victory the Knights built a new fortified capital on the heights of Mount Sciberras, named Valletta after the victorious Grand Master and laid out from 1566 as a planned Renaissance city of palaces, churches and unrivalled fortifications. For two and a half centuries the Order ruled Malta as a small sovereign state, endowing it with auberges, hospitals, the great conventual church of St John and a baroque culture, until the arrival of a new and irresistible power swept the Knights away.

events.title: Charles V grants Malta to the Knights
events.summary: Eight years after losing Rhodes to the Ottomans, the Order of St John received Malta from the Emperor Charles V, who held Sicily, in return for a token annual tribute, and made the islands their new sovereign base.

events.title: The Great Siege of Malta
events.summary: An Ottoman army sent by Suleiman the Magnificent besieged the Knights and Maltese for nearly four months; under Grand Master Jean Parisot de la Valette the defenders held out until a Sicilian relief force compelled the Ottomans to withdraw.

events.title: Founding of Valletta
events.summary: In the year after the siege the Knights began building a new fortified capital on Mount Sciberras, named Valletta after the Grand Master and conceived as a planned Renaissance fortress-city; it became the capital in 1570.

events.title: Two and a half centuries of the Order
events.summary: From their new capital the Knights ruled Malta as a sovereign order-state, building auberges, hospitals and the conventual church of St John and shaping a baroque culture until their fall in 1798.

figures.name: Jean Parisot de la Valette
figures.role: Grand Master of the Knights of St John
figures.blurb: Grand Master during the Great Siege of 1565, de la Valette led the defence of Malta against Suleiman the Magnificent's army and, after the Ottoman withdrawal, founded the new capital that bears his name, Valletta.

figures.name: Philippe de Villiers de l'Isle-Adam
figures.role: Grand Master who took possession of Malta
figures.blurb: The Grand Master who had surrendered Rhodes to the Ottomans in 1522 and, after years without a home, took possession of Malta for the Order in 1530 under the grant of Charles V.

pullquote.text: The superb leadership of Grand Master Jean Parisot de la Valette prevented Suleiman the Magnificent from dislodging the Knights from Malta in 1565, in one of the most famous sieges in history.

eras.title: Napoleon, British Rule and the Naval Fortress
eras.standfirst: Napoleon expelled the Knights in 1798, but a Maltese revolt and British power confirmed British rule by the Treaty of Paris, turning the islands into a great Mediterranean naval base.
body: In June 1798, on his way to Egypt, Napoleon Bonaparte demanded entry to the Grand Harbour and seized Malta from the Knights, whose rule, weakened from within and forbidden to fight fellow Christians, collapsed almost without resistance. The French occupation was brief and unpopular: their attacks on church property and treasure provoked a Maltese uprising within months, and the insurgents, supported by the British and Sicilian fleets, blockaded the French garrison in Valletta until it surrendered in 1800. The Knights would never return as rulers.

Malta's status was settled by the wider Napoleonic settlement. The Maltese, having driven out the French, acknowledged British sovereignty subject to a declaration of their rights, and the islands' attachment to Britain was confirmed by the Treaty of Paris in 1814. Malta became a Crown colony, valued above all for its harbours. British investment in the dockyard and naval facilities of the Grand Harbour made the islands one of the principal bases of the Royal Navy and the strategic linchpin of British power in the Mediterranean.

Through the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries Malta's economy revolved around the imperial garrison and dockyard, and its politics around the slow extension of self-government and recurring tension between Maltese, Italian and British cultural and linguistic loyalties. The islands' position athwart the route between Gibraltar and Suez made them indispensable to the empire, and ensured that when a new general war came to the Mediterranean, Malta would once again find itself in the front line.

events.title: Napoleon seizes Malta
events.summary: On his way to Egypt, Napoleon Bonaparte captured Malta from the Knights of St John, whose rule collapsed almost without a fight, ending two and a half centuries of the Order's sovereignty.

events.title: Maltese uprising and French surrender
events.summary: French seizures of church property triggered a Maltese revolt; with British and Sicilian naval support the insurgents blockaded the French garrison in Valletta until it surrendered in 1800.

events.title: British rule confirmed by the Treaty of Paris
events.summary: Having acknowledged British sovereignty, the Maltese saw the islands' attachment to Britain confirmed by the Treaty of Paris in 1814, making Malta a Crown colony.

events.title: Malta as a naval fortress
events.summary: British development of the Grand Harbour dockyard made Malta one of the chief bases of the Royal Navy and the strategic centre of British power in the Mediterranean.

figures.name: Napoleon Bonaparte
figures.blurb: On his way to Egypt in 1798 Napoleon seized Malta from the Knights of St John, ending the Order's rule. His brief occupation provoked a Maltese revolt that opened the way to British control.

figures.name: Ferdinand von Hompesch
figures.role: Last ruling Grand Master in Malta
figures.blurb: Grand Master of the Order of St John at the time of Napoleon's invasion in 1798, under whom the Knights surrendered Malta to the French, ending the Order's two-and-a-half-century rule of the islands.

pullquote.text: British exploitation of Malta's military facilities dominated the local economy, and the dockyard became the colony's economic mainstay.

eras.title: The George Cross Island and the European Republic
eras.standfirst: Besieged and bombed in the Second World War, Malta won the George Cross for its endurance, then moved from colony to independence, to a neutral republic and finally to membership of the European Union.
body: When Italy entered the Second World War in 1940, Malta, lying between Sicily and the Axis armies in North Africa, became one of the most heavily bombed places on Earth. For more than two years Italian and German aircraft pounded the islands, whose airfields and submarines harried the convoys supplying Rommel's forces. Cut off and starving, Malta was kept alive only by perilous convoys; the arrival of the battered Operation Pedestal convoy, including the crippled tanker Ohio, in August 1942 was the turning point that broke the siege. In recognition of the islanders' endurance, King George VI awarded the George Cross to the whole island on 15 April 1942, an honour unique in being granted to an entire population and still borne on Malta's flag.

After the war Malta moved gradually toward self-rule and then to statehood. On 21 September 1964 the islands became an independent state within the Commonwealth, at first retaining the British monarch as head of state. Ten years later, on 13 December 1974, Malta became a republic with its own president. Under the long dominance of the Labour leader Dom Mintoff, the islands distanced themselves from Britain: the last British forces withdrew on 31 March 1979, commemorated as Freedom Day, and in 1987 a policy of neutrality and non-alignment was written into the constitution.

Freed of its imperial role, Malta reinvented itself as a small, independent European nation. It built an economy on shipping, tourism, manufacturing and financial services, and pursued integration with Europe. On 1 May 2004 Malta joined the European Union in its largest enlargement, and on 1 January 2008 it adopted the euro, completing the islands' transformation from a contested fortress of empires into a sovereign member of the European family of nations.

events.title: The siege of Malta
events.summary: From 1940 Malta, lying between Sicily and Axis North Africa, was subjected to relentless Italian and German bombing and a near-starvation blockade while its forces struck at Axis supply convoys.

events.title: Malta awarded the George Cross
events.summary: King George VI awarded the George Cross to the whole island of Malta to honour its heroism and endurance under siege, the only time the decoration has been given to an entire population.

events.title: Independence
events.summary: Malta became an independent sovereign state within the Commonwealth on 21 September 1964, ending more than a century and a half of British colonial rule.

events.title: Malta becomes a republic
events.summary: Constitutional amendments made Malta a republic on 13 December 1974, replacing the British monarch with a Maltese president as head of state.

events.title: Accession to the European Union
events.summary: Malta joined the European Union on 1 May 2004 in its largest enlargement and adopted the euro on 1 January 2008, completing its integration into Europe.

figures.name: King George VI
figures.blurb: In April 1942 he awarded the George Cross to the island of Malta, declaring that it bore witness to a heroism and devotion that would long be famous in history, the only time the medal has honoured a whole population.

figures.name: Dom Mintoff
figures.blurb: The dominant Labour leader of post-independence Malta who secured the withdrawal of British forces in 1979 and pressed for the islands' neutrality and non-alignment, written into the constitution in 1987.

pullquote.text: To honour her brave people, I award the George Cross to the Island Fortress of Malta to bear witness to a heroism and devotion that will long be famous in history.

figures.name: The temple builders of Malta
figures.blurb: The anonymous Neolithic people who raised the megalithic temples of Ggantija, Hagar Qim, Mnajdra and Tarxien and carved the Hal Saflieni Hypogeum, leaving some of the oldest free-standing monuments on Earth.

figures.name: Jean Parisot de la Valette
figures.role: Grand Master of the Knights of St John
figures.blurb: Grand Master who led the successful defence of Malta in the Great Siege of 1565 against the Ottomans and founded the new capital, Valletta, named in his honour.

figures.name: King George VI
figures.blurb: In 1942 he awarded the George Cross to the whole island of Malta for its endurance under siege in the Second World War, a decoration still borne on the Maltese flag.

figures.name: Dom Mintoff
figures.blurb: The leading figure of post-independence Malta, who oversaw the departure of British forces in 1979 and the entrenchment of constitutional neutrality, shaping the modern republic.

sources.label: Malta: Modern history (British rule and independence)
Roger I, Norman Count of Sicily
History of the Order of Malta: 1048 to Present
George Cross awarded to the island of Malta
